definition: A written contract between an employer (or group of employers) and a union that sets wages, working conditions, and other employment terms for a defined group of workers, usually for a fixed period.
Collective bargaining conducted at industry or sector level rather than at a single company, typically producing agreements that apply to all employers in that sector (or those signatories).
A workplace-level body of elected worker representatives with information, consultation, and in some jurisdictions co-determination rights, distinct from but often coordinated with the union.
Transnational body representing employees of large multinational companies operating in the EU/EEA, with information and consultation rights on cross-border issues (EU Directive 2009/38/EC).
The lowest legal hourly or monthly wage an employer may pay a worker, set by statute or by a binding CBA.
The period during which a worker is at the employer's disposal and carrying out their duties. CBAs commonly set weekly maximums, break rules, and overtime thresholds.
Length of service with an employer, typically used as a basis for entitlements such as pay scale progression, leave accrual, and severance.
Compensation owed to a worker on termination of employment, typically calculated from seniority, salary, and the cause of termination.
An employer's formal acknowledgement of a union as the bargaining representative of a specified group of workers, a prerequisite for enforceable collective bargaining.
Workers' legally protected ability to collectively withhold labour to press demands, subject to national law and often to CBA-level procedural rules (notice, ballots, peace obligations).
